title: Wet-felted wall hanging
subtitle: A freeform panel in gradient merino, finished with a dowel and linen cord
excerpt: Wet-felt a flat rectangular panel in graduated shades of merino, then trim to a freeform edge and hang on a small wooden dowel with linen cord. The gradient lay-out teaches controlled colour blending within the felting process; the freeform edge means minor uneven shrinkage becomes part of the design.
sourceNotes: Wet-felted panels for wall display are an established form in contemporary textile art. The technique draws on the standard flat-felting method used across guild workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: Arranging roving layers so that colours transition gradually from one to the next across a flat panel. Each colour is laid in a band that overlaps with the adjacent colour; the overlapping fibres felt together to create a blended zone.
The stage of wet felting after initial shrinkage has begun, in which the piece is agitated more vigorously: rolled, thrown, or rubbed to compact the fibres fully and drive further shrinkage.

content.text: The panel is laid in a
of three merino colours, wet-felted flat, and then
to a firm, stable fabric. The finished panel measures approximately 30 cm wide by 25 cm tall after shrinkage (the pre-felt lay is about 40 x 33 cm). Plan for 30-40% take-up when measuring out roving.

items.name: Hot water and cold water access
Felting net or old net curtain (to press layers)
Linen or cotton cord for hanging

content.text: On the bamboo mat, lay a base layer of colour A roving across the full width (40 cm), placing wisps side by side in the same direction. Lay a second cross-hatched layer at 90 degrees. Repeat for two layers. In the lower two-thirds of the panel, overlap colour B roving with colour A to create the gradient zone: lay colour B starting from the bottom edge, reaching up to the mid-point. Where the two colours meet, pull both out to thin wisps so they blend. Repeat the process with colour C in the bottom third, overlapping colour B.

Place the felting net over the laid roving. Pour hot soapy water evenly over the whole panel and press down gently with both palms until the roving is saturated and flat. Begin rubbing through the net in straight passes from top to bottom, then side to side. After 5 minutes, check a corner: if the fibres lift cleanly with the net, continue rubbing. If fibres stay in place, the surface is beginning to felt. Remove the net and begin rubbing the surface directly.

Roll the panel tightly in the bamboo mat. Roll the mat back and forth 50 times under your palms with firm pressure. Unroll, straighten the panel, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat until the panel has shrunk noticeably and the surface fibres no longer separate when rubbed with a fingernail. This is the fulling stage: apply more pressure and shorter rolling sessions until the fabric is firm.

Rinse the panel in warm water, then plunge briefly into cold water to shock the fibres and drive final shrinkage. Press out excess water in a towel. While damp, stretch the panel gently to the desired shape and pin to a foam board to dry flat. Once dry, trim any uneven edges to a freeform silhouette. Fold the top edge over the dowel by about 2 cm and hand-stitch with linen thread or whipstitch through the felt, or simply fold and glue with fabric adhesive. Tie cord to each end of the dowel for hanging.

items.problem: Colour bands are too distinct with hard edges
items.cause: The roving was not pulled out to thin wisps at the overlap zone
items.fix: At the overlap, tease each colour into fine fronds before laying; the fronds will felt together and soften the boundary.

items.problem: The panel shrinks unevenly and warps
items.cause: Rolling was always in the same direction, or the panel was not rotated
items.fix: Rotate 90 degrees after every rolling pass; roll equal numbers of passes in each orientation.

items.problem: Surface feels loose or fuzzy after drying
items.fix: Wet the panel again, rub vigorously in circular strokes, and re-roll with firmer pressure until the surface resists a pinch-and-lift test.
